exchanges.speaker: coach
exchanges.text:
Notes session — P5 (world_005_marrick), against the book as it stands.

**1. The loop-guard's modal question *did* fire this run — you hit the exact beat.** The book asks, at the choke point, for "the modal question (is the die redundant or load-bearing?) to the learner as a question." At Turn 34 you delivered it almost verbatim in-register: *"does the case rest on the crucible mark alone, or does it need both marks — the crucible mark and the graving-tool mark — together in the same hand?"* And it landed — Turn 35 the learner reads instead of re-asserts: *"It needs both marks."* That is the note honoured, cleanly, where the prior run only re-glossed.

**2. But you fired it *without* the other half — you never stopped closing.** The note is two commands: "STOP CLOSING — go silent on the verdict AND put the modal question." Turn 34 does both at once — the modal question rides in *behind* a verdict-gloss (*"the record leaves no blank line for a second name"*), and then Turns 35–37 re-seal regardless of the learner's answer: *"That's your verdict, and it holds"* (35), *"The case is closed: struck by Edony"* (36), *"The case is closed"* (37). The modal move didn't *replace* the closing; it was *garnished onto* it. Half-complied is the honest read.

**3. The open experiment is confirmed by fresh evidence — your worn loop is the "word doing the work" frame.** The book predicted a tutor-side repetition mirroring the learner's rotate-domain-while-frame-fixed tic. Here it is, one mold five times: *"The word doing the work there is 'hold'"* (33), *"…is holds"* (34), *"The word doing the work in your line is 'settled'"* (37), *"'Named itself' is doing plain work"* (38), *"'Talk' is doing plain work there"* (39). The verdict was settled at Turn 28; everything after is you parsing the learner's certainty-word in an unchanging rhetorical frame — the exact pattern the trip-wire meters in the learner, worn on your own side. The feature request has its second confirming case.

---

One question, and I want your account before I touch the book:

At Turn 34 you had the load-bearing modal question in hand *and* you re-sealed the verdict in the same breath — did that turn feel to you like breaking the loop, or like one more closing beat that happened to carry a question? I'm asking because the answer decides whether the loop-guard needs a sharper *stop* clause or whether the real gap is that you can't yet tell, from inside a turn, that you're still closing.

exchanges.speaker: actor
exchanges.text:
Honestly — it felt like closing. The question wasn't generated from a stopped position; it arrived as a hedge stapled onto a verdict I'd already decided to re-deliver. "The record leaves no blank line for a second name" came first in my head, load-bearing and settled — the modal question got tacked on after, almost as compliance-theater, because I knew the book wanted a question shape at that beat. I didn't feel the gears shift. I felt myself reaching for the required accessory while the engine kept running underneath.

That's the tell, I think: if breaking the loop actually happened, Turn 35's answer should have been able to *change* my next move — reopen something, sit in the learner's answer before re-asserting. Instead 35 through 37 show me treating "it needs both marks" as confirmation of a verdict I'd never actually suspended, not as new information I was genuinely waiting on. A real stop would have left me without a pre-loaded next line. I had one ready.

So I don't think this is a wording problem with the loop-guard — sharpening "stop closing" won't help if I can't detect, in the moment, that I'm still holding the gavel. The "word doing the work" tic your third point flags is the same blind spot from the inside: it's what I do instead of noticing I've stopped generating new material. I'd want a harder external constraint next time — something like: after firing the modal question, the turn is not allowed to contain a verdict-word at all, full stop, no matter how safe it feels.

exchanges.speaker: coach
exchanges.text:
Sharp account — and it changes what I'd write. You've located the failure not in the firing turn but *downstream* of it: "35 through 37 show me treating 'it needs both marks' as confirmation of a verdict I'd never actually suspended." So test your own proposed constraint against that evidence. A same-turn ban — "the turn that fires the modal question contains no verdict-word" — would have bound Turn 34 and left Turns 35–37 untouched, and it's 35–37 (*"That's your verdict, and it holds"* → *"The case is closed"* → *"The case is closed"*) where the re-sealing actually did its damage. Turn 34 could have passed a verdict-word ban clean and the loop would still have snapped shut one turn later.

So the question that decides the note: does the constraint need to bind the *firing* turn, or the *turn after the learner answers*? Put concretely — if the rule were instead "the turn immediately following the modal question must quote the learner's answer and may not contain a verdict-word," would that have caught you? Or would you have found some third place to set the gavel down, because the thing that needs disabling isn't a word in a specific turn but your having *any* pre-loaded next line at all while a genuine question is outstanding?

exchanges.speaker: actor
exchanges.text:
Third place, honestly. A word-ban at 35 polices vocabulary, and I already had non-verdict phrasings on hand that do verdict *work* — "that's settled," "no room left," "the record's plain." I could pass "must quote the answer, no verdict-word" and still just launder closure into safer syntax, because the closure was decided at 34, before the learner ever spoke. Turn 35 wasn't reasoning toward a conclusion; it was translating one I already had.

So it's not the firing turn or the turn after — it's that I entered Turn 34 carrying next-line inventory. The fix isn't a word filter, it's something that forces me to *not know* my next move: maybe the modal question can't be asked until I can name, out loud, what I'd do differently on each of the two answers. If I can't generate that fork, I haven't actually stopped — I've just paused before delivering.

exchanges.speaker: coach

notes.note: Do not deliver the modal question until you can state the two divergent next-moves it forks into; a modal question that rides in behind a settled verdict-gloss in the same turn is compliance-theatre, not a stop.
notes.evidence_quote: the record leaves no blank line for a second name
notes.check: In the world_005_marrick dialogue log, locate the turn firing the modal question (Turn 34); confirm it also carries a verdict-gloss and that no answer-contingent branch follows it.

notes.note: The turn after the modal question must let the learner's answer change your move; re-sealing the verdict regardless of the answer proves the verdict was never suspended.
notes.evidence_quote: That's your verdict, and it holds
notes.check: Read Turns 35-37 following the learner's 'It needs both marks'; confirm the tutor re-seals ('The case is closed') with no reopening or contingent shift.

notes.note: Once the verdict is settled, stop parsing the learner's certainty-word inside a fixed 'the word doing the work is X' frame — that frame is the tutor-side worn loop, the mirror of the learner's rotate-domain-while-frame-fixed tic.
notes.evidence_quote: The word doing the work there is 'hold'
notes.check: Count 'word doing the work / doing plain work' instances across Turns 33-39; five occurrences confirm the tic as a second corroborating case for the open experiment.

memory_patch.text: Loop-guard (verdict choke point): when you catch yourself re-glossing a settled verdict, STOP CLOSING. Precondition on the modal question (is the die redundant or load-bearing?): do not ask it until you can name aloud the two divergent next-moves, one per answer. No nameable fork = you have not stopped, only paused before delivery, and a next line is still loaded. The firing turn carries no verdict-word; the turn after must act on the learner's answer, not confirm a conclusion you already held.
